answer:
Debugging a Real Express Middleware Issue in an Interview Round

1. Definition & Context

Express Middleware functions are functions that execute in sequence during the request-response cycle, each receiving req, res, and next. Debugging middleware issues means isolating which middleware in the chain is misbehaving — swallowing errors, mutating req incorrectly, calling next() multiple times, or silently failing without forwarding control. In an interview, the key is demonstrating a structured approach: reproduce, isolate the layer, inspect state at each boundary, and prove the fix with a test.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the Express.js backend used a middleware chain: cors → bodyParser → authMiddleware → roleGuard → route handler. During a sprint demo, the sales team reported that creating new leads returned 403 Forbidden for all users — even admins. The issue was traced to the roleGuard middleware, which had been refactored the same morning. The developer changed req.user.role to req.user.roles (plural, as an array) in the auth middleware, but the roleGuard still checked the old singular role property. Since req.user.role was undefined, the guard rejected every request.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — broken roleGuard middleware
function roleGuard(...allowedRoles) {
  return (req, res, next) => {
    // BUG: req.user.role is undefined because authMiddleware
    // now sets req.user.roles (array) instead of req.user.role (string)
    if (!allowedRoles.includes(req.user.role)) {
      return res.status(403).json({ error: 'Insufficient permissions' });
    }
    next();
  };
}

// AFTER — fixed to handle roles array
function roleGuard(...allowedRoles) {
  return (req, res, next) => {
    const userRoles = req.user?.roles || [];
    const hasPermission = userRoles.some(r => allowedRoles.includes(r));
    if (!hasPermission) {
      console.warn(
        `[roleGuard] Denied: user=${req.user?.sub}, ` +
        `has=[${userRoles}], needs=[${allowedRoles}]`
      );
      return res.status(403).json({
        error: 'Insufficient permissions',
        required: allowedRoles,
      });
    }
    next();
  };
}

// Diagnostic middleware — insert temporarily to trace the chain
function debugMiddleware(label) {
  return (req, res, next) => {
    console.log(`[${label}] req.user:`, JSON.stringify(req.user));
    console.log(`[${label}] headers.authorization:`,
      req.headers.authorization?.substring(0, 30) + '...');
    const originalSend = res.send;
    res.send = function (body) {
      console.log(`[${label}] response status: ${res.statusCode}`);
      return originalSend.call(this, body);
    };
    next();
  };
}

// Usage: insert between middlewares to trace
app.use('/api/leads',
  debugMiddleware('pre-auth'),
  authMiddleware,
  debugMiddleware('post-auth'),
  roleGuard('sales', 'admin'),
  debugMiddleware('post-guard'),
  leadsRouter
);

// Regression test with supertest
import request from 'supertest';
import { app } from '../app';
import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';

describe('roleGuard middleware', () => {
  it('allows users with matching role in roles array', async () => {
    const token = jwt.sign(
      { sub: 'user-1', roles: ['sales'] },
      process.env.JWT_SECRET
    );
    const res = await request(app)
      .post('/api/leads')
      .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
      .send({ name: 'Test Lead', email: 'test@example.com' });
    expect(res.status).not.toBe(403);
  });

  it('rejects users without any matching role', async () => {
    const token = jwt.sign(
      { sub: 'user-2', roles: ['viewer'] },
      process.env.JWT_SECRET
    );
    const res = await request(app)
      .post('/api/leads')
      .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`);
    expect(res.status).toBe(403);
    expect(res.body.required).toContain('sales');
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj described this incident step by step:

  1. Symptom: Sales team reported "Forbidden" on lead creation during a sprint demo. Quick check confirmed admins got the same error — so it wasn't role-specific, it was universal.
  2. Isolation: Inserted debugMiddleware loggers between each middleware. Logs revealed that after authMiddleware, req.user had { sub: 'user-1', roles: ['admin'] } — note the plural roles.
  3. Root cause: Checked git diff HEAD~1 -- src/middleware/auth.ts and saw the morning refactor changed req.user = { role: decoded.role } to req.user = { roles: decoded.roles } to support multi-role users, but the roleGuard file wasn't updated.
  4. Fix: Updated roleGuard to use .roles (array) with .some() check. Added a TypeScript interface RequestUser { sub: string; roles: string[] } shared between auth and guard middleware, so future shape mismatches would be caught at compile time.
  5. Prevention: Created an integration test that verifies the auth → roleGuard pipeline for each role type. Added this test to the CI pipeline so shape mismatches break the build.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Middleware A mutates req shape without updating downstream middleware BDefine a shared TypeScript interface for req.user — both auth and guard import it. Compile-time errors catch mismatches before runtime.
Calling next() after already sending a response causes "headers already sent" crashAlways return before next() or res.send(). Add an ESLint rule no-unreachable and custom lint check for res.send without return.
Error-handling middleware placed before route handlers is silently skippedExpress error handlers (4 args: err, req, res, next) must be registered after all routes. Add a startup self-test that verifies error handler is last in the stack.
Async middleware errors crash the server with unhandled promise rejectionWrap all async middleware with asyncHandler wrapper: (fn) => (req, res, next) => Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next)

7. Validation Strategy

  • Middleware chain test: Integration test that exercises the full chain — auth → roleGuard → handler — for every role permutation (sales, admin, viewer, unauthenticated)
  • Contract test: Assert that authMiddleware output matches the RequestUser interface using a runtime schema validator (Zod) in test mode
  • Monkey-patch detection: In CI, verify that res.send is called exactly once per request using a custom middleware that counts invocations
  • Error propagation test: Throw an error inside a route handler, verify the error-handling middleware catches it and returns a structured JSON error (not an HTML stack trace)

Comparing Two Approaches in Express Middleware with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

When building Express middleware, two common architectural approaches emerge: Approach A — Monolithic middleware chain where each concern (auth, validation, rate-limiting, logging) is a separate function composed via app.use() in sequence, versus Approach B — Decorator/wrapper pattern where middleware logic is encapsulated into higher-order functions that wrap route handlers, bundling concerns like auth + validation into a single composable unit. The choice impacts testability, reusability, and debugging clarity.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the Express.js backend had 25+ route handlers across leads, deals, and activities modules. Initially, Manoj used Approach A — a linear middleware chain:

app.use(cors());
app.use(express.json());
app.use(authMiddleware);
app.use('/api/leads', roleGuard('sales', 'admin'), leadsRouter);
app.use('/api/deals', roleGuard('sales', 'manager'), dealsRouter);

This worked until requirements diverged: some lead routes needed rate-limiting but deals didn't, some activities endpoints were public (webhooks) while others needed auth. The chain became fragile — reordering one app.use() broke unrelated routes.

Manoj migrated to Approach B — a protect() wrapper that bundled per-route concerns:

router.post('/leads', protect({ roles: ['sales', 'admin'], rateLimit: 30, validate: leadSchema }), createLead);

Each route explicitly declared its requirements, making it self-documenting and independently testable.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// ========== APPROACH A: Linear Middleware Chain ==========
import express from 'express';
import cors from 'cors';
import { authMiddleware, roleGuard, rateLimiter } from './middleware';
import leadsRouter from './routes/leads';
import dealsRouter from './routes/deals';

const app = express();

// Global chain — order is critical
app.use(cors());
app.use(express.json({ limit: '5mb' }));
app.use(authMiddleware);           // all routes require auth
app.use(rateLimiter({ max: 100 })); // global rate limit

// Route-level guards
app.use('/api/leads', roleGuard('sales', 'admin'), leadsRouter);
app.use('/api/deals', roleGuard('sales', 'manager'), dealsRouter);
// Problem: webhook route also needs auth skipped, but it's global
app.use('/api/webhooks', webhooksRouter); // ← still requires auth!


// ========== APPROACH B: Wrapper/Decorator Pattern ==========
import express from 'express';
import cors from 'cors';
import { protect, publicRoute } from './middleware/protect';
import { leadSchema, dealSchema } from './schemas';

const app = express();
app.use(cors());
app.use(express.json({ limit: '5mb' }));

// Each route declares its own requirements
const leadsRouter = express.Router();
leadsRouter.get('/',
  protect({ roles: ['sales', 'admin'], rateLimit: 100 }),
  listLeads
);
leadsRouter.post('/',
  protect({ roles: ['sales', 'admin'], rateLimit: 30, validate: leadSchema }),
  createLead
);

const webhooksRouter = express.Router();
webhooksRouter.post('/crm-sync',
  publicRoute({ rateLimit: 10, validate: webhookSchema }), // no auth
  handleWebhook
);

// protect() implementation
function protect(options) {
  const middleware = [];

  // Auth — always included unless explicitly skipped
  middleware.push(authMiddleware);

  // Role check
  if (options.roles?.length) {
    middleware.push((req, res, next) => {
      const hasRole = req.user.roles.some(r => options.roles.includes(r));
      if (!hasRole) return res.status(403).json({ error: 'Forbidden' });
      next();
    });
  }

  // Rate limiting
  if (options.rateLimit) {
    middleware.push(rateLimiter({ max: options.rateLimit }));
  }

  // Validation
  if (options.validate) {
    middleware.push((req, res, next) => {
      const result = options.validate.safeParse(req.body);
      if (!result.success) {
        return res.status(400).json({ errors: result.error.flatten() });
      }
      req.body = result.data; // sanitized
      next();
    });
  }

  return middleware; // Express accepts arrays of middleware
}

function publicRoute(options) {
  const middleware = [];
  if (options.rateLimit) middleware.push(rateLimiter({ max: options.rateLimit }));
  if (options.validate) middleware.push(/* ... same validation ... */);
  return middleware;
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj initially used Approach A for the CRM Dashboard and hit a painful bug: a new /api/webhooks/crm-sync endpoint for a third-party CRM integration was added below the global app.use(authMiddleware) line. The webhook had no Bearer token (it used HMAC signature verification instead), so every webhook call returned 401. The fix required restructuring the entire middleware order — moving authMiddleware from global to per-router scope — which risked breaking existing routes.

This incident motivated the migration to Approach B. With the wrapper pattern, the webhook route simply used publicRoute() instead of protect(), and no other routes were affected. The migration took one afternoon: Manoj wrote a script that listed all routes and their middleware requirements, then converted each to use the protect() wrapper. He verified the migration by running the existing integration test suite — all 47 tests passed without changes, confirming behavioral equivalence.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Approach A: Adding a public route below global auth makes it unexpectedly protectedUse Approach B or explicitly mount public routes BEFORE the global auth middleware. Document the ordering constraint in a comment block.
Approach B: protect() wrapper becomes a "god function" with too many optionsLimit wrapper to 4-5 orthogonal concerns (auth, roles, rate-limit, validate). For anything else, compose a separate middleware explicitly.
Approach A: Middleware executes even for 404 routes, wasting resourcesRegister route-specific middleware on routers, not on app. Use router.use() to scope middleware to routes that actually exist.
Both: Middleware errors are silently swallowed if next(err) isn't calledWrap all middleware in asyncHandler and register a global error-handling middleware that logs and returns structured errors.

7. Validation Strategy

  • Route audit test: Script that iterates app._router.stack and verifies every non-public route has auth middleware in its chain — fails if a route is accidentally unprotected
  • Behavioral equivalence test: When migrating from A to B, run the full integration suite without changes — all tests must pass, proving the wrapper produces the same behavior
  • Per-route isolation test: For Approach B, test protect({ roles: ['admin'] }) with mock req/res/next — verify it calls next only when role matches, returns 403 otherwise
  • Performance benchmark: Compare response times for 1000 requests between Approach A (global middleware runs for all) and Approach B (only relevant middleware runs) — wrapper pattern should be equal or faster for routes with fewer concerns

Debugging a Real Fastify Plugins Issue in an Interview Round

1. Definition & Context

Fastify Plugins are encapsulated units of functionality registered via fastify.register(). Each plugin creates its own encapsulation context — decorators, hooks, and routes registered inside a plugin are scoped to that plugin and its children, not visible to siblings or parents. This encapsulation is powerful but creates a unique class of bugs: decorators not visible where expected, hooks running in unexpected order, and plugin registration race conditions. Debugging requires understanding Fastify's directed acyclic graph (DAG) of plugin contexts.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Fastify backend was organized into plugins: authPlugin (JWT verification + decorateRequest('user')), leavePlugin (leave CRUD routes), notifyPlugin (Socket.io notifications), and approvalPlugin (manager approval chain). During development, Manoj registered all plugins at the top level. When he later moved authPlugin into a registerAuth wrapper to add prefix scoping, the leavePlugin suddenly threw: "The decorator 'user' is not present in the request". The cause was that authPlugin was now encapsulated inside the wrapper, so its decorateRequest was no longer visible to sibling plugins.

3. Plugin Encapsulation Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — authPlugin wrapped in encapsulation (BUG)
import fp from 'fastify-plugin';

// auth.plugin.ts — registers decorator inside encapsulated scope
async function authPlugin(fastify, opts) {
  fastify.decorateRequest('user', null);
  fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
    try {
      const decoded = await req.jwtVerify();
      req.user = decoded;
    } catch (err) {
      reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
    }
  });
}
// BUG: exporting without fp() — encapsulated!
export default authPlugin;

// app.ts — registration
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import jwt from '@fastify/jwt';
import authPlugin from './plugins/auth.plugin';
import leavePlugin from './plugins/leave.plugin';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
app.register(jwt, { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET });
app.register(authPlugin);  // encapsulated → decorator invisible
app.register(leavePlugin); // CRASH: 'user' decorator not found


// AFTER — use fastify-plugin (fp) to break encapsulation
async function authPlugin(fastify, opts) {
  fastify.decorateRequest('user', null);
  fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
    // Skip public routes
    if (req.routeOptions?.config?.isPublic) return;
    try {
      const decoded = await req.jwtVerify();
      req.user = decoded;
    } catch (err) {
      reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
    }
  });
}
// FIX: wrap with fp() to expose to parent scope
export default fp(authPlugin, {
  name: 'auth-plugin',
  dependencies: ['@fastify/jwt'],
});

// leave.plugin.ts — now has access to req.user
async function leavePlugin(fastify, opts) {
  fastify.get('/api/leave/balance', async (req, reply) => {
    const userId = req.user.sub; // ✓ works because auth broke encapsulation
    const balance = await getBalance(userId);
    return { balance };
  });
}
export default leavePlugin; // stays encapsulated — its routes are scoped

// Diagnostic: print the plugin tree
app.ready().then(() => {
  console.log(app.printPlugins());
  // Shows the DAG — verify authPlugin is at root level
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj described this debugging process in detail:

  1. Error: After refactoring the plugin structure, the Fastify server crashed on startup with FST_ERR_DEC_MISSING — "The decorator 'user' is not present in the request."
  2. First hypothesis: Plugin registration order was wrong. Manoj reordered app.register() calls — same error. This ruled out ordering since Fastify handles async registration order via its DAG.
  3. Key insight: Used app.printPlugins() after app.ready() to visualize the plugin tree. Saw that authPlugin was nested under a wrapper scope, while leavePlugin was at root. Decorators don't flow sideways between sibling scopes.
  4. Fix: Wrapped authPlugin export with fp() (fastify-plugin). This tells Fastify to "hoist" the plugin's decorators and hooks to the parent scope. After the fix, app.printPlugins() showed auth decorators at root level.
  5. Validation: Added a test that calls app.inject() on a protected route and verifies req.user is populated. Also added a ready handler that checks app.hasRequestDecorator('user') — if false, the server refuses to start with a clear error message.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Forgetting fp() wrapper causes decorators to be invisible to sibling pluginsConvention: any plugin that adds decorators or hooks shared across the app MUST use fp(). Add an ESLint comment rule or PR checklist item for this.
Using fp() on route plugins accidentally leaks routes to parent scopeOnly use fp() for utility/decorator plugins. Route plugins should stay encapsulated so prefix scoping works correctly.
Plugin dependency not declared — registration order becomes fragileAlways declare dependencies in fp() options: fp(plugin, { dependencies: ['@fastify/jwt'] }). Fastify will throw a clear error if the dependency isn't registered.
Async plugin await not used during registration — race condition on startupUse await app.register(plugin) or rely on app.after() callbacks. Always call await app.ready() before starting the server.

7. Validation Strategy

  • Plugin tree snapshot test: Call app.printPlugins() in CI, snapshot the output, and diff against expected structure — catches accidental scope changes
  • Decorator presence test: In a ready hook, assert app.hasRequestDecorator('user') and app.hasDecorator('prisma') — fail fast if encapsulation hid them
  • Integration test per plugin: Use app.inject() to send requests to each plugin's routes, verify they can access shared decorators and hooks
  • Dependency graph test: Verify that fp() plugins declare all dependencies, and that removing any plugin produces a clear dependency error (not a silent undefined)

Comparing Two Approaches in Fastify Plugins with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

When structuring a Fastify application, two plugin architectures emerge: Approach A — Flat plugin registration where all plugins are registered at the root Fastify instance with prefix-based routing, versus Approach B — Nested plugin hierarchy where plugins are organized into parent-child trees, with parent plugins registering child plugins inside their own encapsulated context. The choice affects encapsulation boundaries, decorator visibility, hook inheritance, and team scalability.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Fastify backend started with Approach A: all plugins registered at root level with prefixes:

app.register(authPlugin);
app.register(leavePlugin, { prefix: '/api/leave' });
app.register(approvalPlugin, { prefix: '/api/approval' });
app.register(notifyPlugin, { prefix: '/api/notify' });

This was simple but caused issues when notifyPlugin needed Socket.io decorator access but approvalPlugin didn't — the Socket.io decorator was visible everywhere, polluting plugins that didn't need it.

Manoj migrated to Approach B — a nested hierarchy where a realtimePlugin parent contained both notifyPlugin and socketPlugin, while leavePlugin and approvalPlugin stayed at root scope with only auth access. This gave each subtree exactly the decorators it needed, nothing more.

3. Architecture Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// ========== APPROACH A: Flat Registration ==========
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import fp from 'fastify-plugin';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });

// All at root — every plugin sees everything
app.register(require('@fastify/jwt'), { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET });
app.register(fp(authPlugin));     // fp() → decorators at root
app.register(fp(socketPlugin));   // fp() → Socket.io at root
app.register(leaveRoutes, { prefix: '/api/leave' });
app.register(approvalRoutes, { prefix: '/api/approval' });
app.register(notifyRoutes, { prefix: '/api/notify' });
// Issue: leaveRoutes can accidentally access socketPlugin's io decorator


// ========== APPROACH B: Nested Hierarchy ==========
const app = Fastify({ logger: true });

// Shared utilities — available everywhere via fp()
app.register(require('@fastify/jwt'), { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET });
app.register(fp(authPlugin));

// REST API subtree — only has auth, no Socket.io
app.register(async function apiSubtree(api) {
  api.register(leaveRoutes, { prefix: '/api/leave' });
  api.register(approvalRoutes, { prefix: '/api/approval' });
});

// Realtime subtree — has auth + Socket.io
app.register(async function realtimeSubtree(rt) {
  // Socket.io only visible within this subtree
  rt.register(socketPlugin); // NOT fp() → stays encapsulated
  rt.register(notifyRoutes, { prefix: '/api/notify' });

  // notify can access rt.io but leaveRoutes cannot
  rt.addHook('onRequest', async (req) => {
    req.io = rt.io; // inject Socket.io into realtime requests only
  });
});

// socketPlugin.ts — encapsulated decorator
async function socketPlugin(fastify) {
  const io = require('socket.io')(fastify.server, {
    cors: { origin: process.env.CLIENT_URL },
  });
  fastify.decorate('io', io);
  io.on('connection', (socket) => {
    fastify.log.info(`Socket connected: ${socket.id}`);
  });
}

// notifyRoutes.ts — uses socket from parent scope
async function notifyRoutes(fastify) {
  fastify.post('/broadcast', async (req, reply) => {
    const { event, data } = req.body;
    // Access io from the realtimeSubtree's scope
    fastify.io.emit(event, data); // ✓ available
    return { sent: true };
  });
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

When the HR Leave System used Approach A, Manoj discovered a subtle performance issue: the authPlugin preHandler hook ran on every request, including Socket.io upgrade requests and health checks. The JWT verification added 15ms to each request, and under load (500+ concurrent WebSocket connections during shift changes), this created noticeable latency.

After migrating to Approach B (nested hierarchy), the auth preHandler only ran inside the apiSubtree, not inside realtimeSubtree (where Socket.io used its own authentication during the handshake). This reduced unnecessary JWT verifications by 40% during peak periods. Manoj confirmed the improvement by comparing Fastify's built-in --verbose logger output before and after — hook execution counts per route were now exactly as expected.

The migration itself had one hiccup: approvalRoutes tried to emit a Socket.io event when a leave was approved (fastify.io.emit('leave:approved')), but after nesting, io wasn't in the API subtree's scope. Manoj solved this by publishing an event to an in-process EventEmitter that the realtime subtree listened to — decoupling the subtrees cleanly.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Nested hierarchy makes cross-subtree communication harder (API subtree can't emit Socket.io events)Use an in-process EventEmitter or a message broker (Redis Pub/Sub) to decouple subtrees. Register the emitter as a root-level decorator via fp().
Developers accidentally use fp() on route plugins, breaking encapsulationTeam convention: only files named *.shared.ts use fp(). Add a lint rule that flags fp() usage in files without the .shared suffix.
Flat approach makes hooks run on all routes, wasting CPU on irrelevant pathsMigrate to nested approach or use route-level config flags (config: { skipAuth: true }) checked inside the hook to short-circuit.
Plugin dependency errors are cryptic when nesting is deepAlways pass dependencies array to fp(). Use app.printPlugins() in development to visualize and verify the tree.

7. Validation Strategy

  • Encapsulation test: In the API subtree, assert that fastify.hasDecorator('io') returns false — proves Socket.io isn't leaking
  • Hook scope test: Add a counter in the auth preHandler, fire 100 requests to API routes and 100 to health check — verify counter increments only for API routes
  • Cross-subtree communication test: Approve a leave via API, verify Socket.io event is received by a test client — validates the EventEmitter bridge works
  • Plugin tree snapshot: Save app.printPlugins() output in CI and diff against a committed snapshot — detects unintended structural changes

Comparing Two Approaches in JWT Lifecycle with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

The JWT lifecycle encompasses token creation, transmission, verification, renewal, and revocation. Two dominant approaches exist: Approach A — Short-lived access token only (e.g., 15-minute JWT, user re-authenticates on expiry) versus Approach B — Access + Refresh token pair (short-lived access token + long-lived refresh token stored in an httpOnly cookie, enabling silent renewal). The choice trades off between simplicity and user experience, security surface and complexity of revocation.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the initial implementation used Approach A — a 30-minute JWT. Employees filling out multi-step leave forms (selecting dates, uploading medical documents, adding notes) often exceeded 30 minutes. The token expired mid-submission, losing their form data and forcing re-login. HR managers reviewing 20+ leave requests in one session had to log in repeatedly, creating friction.

Manoj migrated to Approach B — a 15-minute access token paired with a 7-day refresh token stored in an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie. The Angular SPA's HTTP interceptor detected 401 responses, silently called POST /api/auth/refresh to get a new access token, then retried the failed request. The user never saw a login page during normal usage, while the access token's short life limited the window for stolen-token attacks.

3. Token Lifecycle Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// ========== APPROACH A: Short-lived Access Token Only ==========
// auth.routes.ts (Fastify)
app.post('/api/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
  if (!user || !await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash)) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  }
  // Single token — 30-minute lifespan
  const token = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user.id, roles: user.roles },
    { expiresIn: '30m' }
  );
  return { token };
  // Problem: user must re-login every 30 minutes
});


// ========== APPROACH B: Access + Refresh Token Pair ==========
// auth.routes.ts (Fastify)
app.post('/api/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
  if (!user || !await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash)) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  }

  // Short-lived access token
  const accessToken = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user.id, roles: user.roles, type: 'access' },
    { expiresIn: '15m' }
  );

  // Long-lived refresh token — stored in DB for revocation
  const refreshToken = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user.id, type: 'refresh', jti: crypto.randomUUID() },
    { expiresIn: '7d' }
  );

  // Store refresh token hash in DB (for revocation)
  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      userId: user.id,
      tokenHash: crypto.createHash('sha256')
        .update(refreshToken).digest('hex'),
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000),
    },
  });

  // Set refresh token as httpOnly cookie
  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', refreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true,
    secure: true,
    sameSite: 'strict',
    path: '/api/auth/refresh',
    maxAge: 7 * 24 * 60 * 60, // 7 days
  });

  return { accessToken };
});

// Silent refresh endpoint
app.post('/api/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (!refreshToken) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'No refresh token' });
  }

  try {
    const decoded = app.jwt.verify(refreshToken);
    if (decoded.type !== 'refresh') throw new Error('Wrong token type');

    // Check if token is revoked
    const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256')
      .update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
    const stored = await prisma.refreshToken.findFirst({
      where: { tokenHash, userId: decoded.sub },
    });
    if (!stored) {
      return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Token revoked' });
    }

    // Rotate: delete old, issue new refresh + access
    await prisma.refreshToken.delete({ where: { id: stored.id } });

    const newAccessToken = app.jwt.sign(
      { sub: decoded.sub, roles: decoded.roles, type: 'access' },
      { expiresIn: '15m' }
    );
    const newRefreshToken = app.jwt.sign(
      { sub: decoded.sub, type: 'refresh', jti: crypto.randomUUID() },
      { expiresIn: '7d' }
    );

    await prisma.refreshToken.create({
      data: {
        userId: decoded.sub,
        tokenHash: crypto.createHash('sha256')
          .update(newRefreshToken).digest('hex'),
        expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000),
      },
    });

    reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
      httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict',
      path: '/api/auth/refresh', maxAge: 7 * 24 * 60 * 60,
    });

    return { accessToken: newAccessToken };
  } catch (err) {
    reply.clearCookie('refreshToken');
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid refresh token' });
  }
});

// Angular interceptor — silent refresh
@Injectable()
export class AuthInterceptor implements HttpInterceptor {
  private refreshing = false;
  private queue: Subject<string> = new Subject();

  intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
    const token = this.authService.getAccessToken();
    const authReq = token
      ? req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } })
      : req;

    return next.handle(authReq).pipe(
      catchError(err => {
        if (err.status === 401 && !req.url.includes('/auth/refresh')) {
          return this.handleRefresh(req, next);
        }
        return throwError(() => err);
      })
    );
  }

  private handleRefresh(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
    if (!this.refreshing) {
      this.refreshing = true;
      return this.authService.refresh().pipe(
        switchMap(({ accessToken }) => {
          this.refreshing = false;
          this.authService.setAccessToken(accessToken);
          this.queue.next(accessToken);
          return next.handle(req.clone({
            setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}` },
          }));
        })
      );
    }
    // Queue concurrent requests until refresh completes
    return this.queue.pipe(
      take(1),
      switchMap(token => next.handle(req.clone({
        setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` },
      })))
    );
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After migrating to Approach B in the HR Leave System, Manoj hit a critical bug: concurrent API calls during token refresh caused a race condition. When the access token expired, the Angular SPA had 3 parallel requests in flight (leave balance, pending approvals, notifications). All three received 401, and all three triggered /auth/refresh simultaneously. The first refresh succeeded and rotated the token, but the second and third refresh requests used the now-deleted old refresh token — returning 401 and logging the user out.

Manoj debugged this by:

  1. Adding console.log in the interceptor — saw 3 concurrent refresh calls.
  2. Checking Fastify logs — the first /auth/refresh succeeded (200), the second and third failed (401) because the old token was already rotated.
  3. Fix: Added a refreshing flag and a Subject queue in the Angular interceptor. The first 401 triggers the refresh; subsequent 401s wait for the refresh to complete, then retry with the new token. This pattern is called a "refresh token gate."
  4. Server-side fix: Added a 10-second grace window — if a refresh token was just rotated, the old token hash is kept in a short-lived cache. If the same old token is used within 10 seconds, it returns the same new tokens instead of revoking. This handles edge cases where the queue pattern fails.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Concurrent requests during refresh cause multiple rotation attempts, logging user outImplement a client-side refresh gate (queue pattern). Server-side: add a 10-second grace window for recently-rotated tokens.
Refresh token stored in localStorage is vulnerable to XSSStore refresh token ONLY in httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie. Never expose it to JavaScript.
No revocation mechanism — stolen refresh token valid for 7 daysStore refresh token hashes in DB. On logout, delete all user's refresh tokens. On password change, invalidate all existing tokens.
Access token still valid after user role change (e.g., employee promoted to manager)Keep access token TTL short (15m) so role changes propagate within one cycle. For immediate revocation, maintain a short-lived deny-list in Redis checked in the auth hook.

7. Validation Strategy

  • Silent refresh test: Playwright test that logs in, waits 15 minutes (or mocks clock), performs an action, and verifies no login redirect — the interceptor silently refreshed
  • Concurrent request test: Fire 5 API calls simultaneously after token expiry — verify only 1 refresh request hits the server, and all 5 original requests eventually succeed
  • Revocation test: Log in on two devices, logout on device A, verify device B's next refresh fails with 401 (token was revoked)
  • Cookie security test: Verify refresh token cookie has httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict flags using Playwright's context.cookies() inspection
  • Token rotation test: After refresh, verify the old refresh token no longer works (one-time use enforcement)

Designing a Practical JWT Lifecycle Implementation for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

A complete JWT lifecycle implementation for a mid-size product covers: (1) Issuance — minting tokens at login with appropriate claims and expiry, (2) Transport — secure transmission via headers and cookies, (3) Verification — validating signature, expiry, and claims on each request, (4) Renewal — silent token refresh for seamless UX, (5) Revocation — invalidating tokens on logout, password change, or security events, and (6) Rotation — periodic key rotation without downtime. For a mid-size product like an HR management system with 50-200 users, the design must be secure enough to handle sensitive employee data while remaining simple enough for a small team to maintain.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the JWT lifecycle handled three user roles (employee, manager, admin) with different permission levels. The system managed sensitive data — leave balances, medical certificates, salary deduction implications — requiring strong auth. The implementation used Fastify with @fastify/jwt on the backend and Angular 18 with an HTTP interceptor on the frontend.

Key design decisions: (1) Access tokens carried role and department claims so the Fastify auth hook could make authorization decisions without DB lookups. (2) Refresh tokens were stored in PostgreSQL with Prisma, enabling per-device revocation (an employee could log out from their phone without affecting their desktop session). (3) Socket.io connections authenticated during handshake using the access token, and reconnection attempts included the latest access token from the interceptor's state.

3. JWT Lifecycle Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// ==========================================
// 1. Token Issuance — login.ts (Fastify)
// ==========================================
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import jwt from '@fastify/jwt';
import cookie from '@fastify/cookie';
import bcrypt from 'bcrypt';
import crypto from 'crypto';
import { prisma } from './db';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
app.register(jwt, { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET! });
app.register(cookie);

app.post('/api/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const { email, password, deviceId } = req.body as any;
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
  if (!user || !await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash)) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  }

  // Access token — short-lived, carries authorization claims
  const accessToken = app.jwt.sign({
    sub: user.id,
    roles: user.roles,          // ['employee'] or ['manager', 'employee']
    department: user.department, // 'engineering'
    type: 'access',
  }, { expiresIn: '15m' });

  // Refresh token — long-lived, minimal claims
  const jti = crypto.randomUUID();
  const refreshToken = app.jwt.sign({
    sub: user.id,
    type: 'refresh',
    jti,
    deviceId: deviceId || 'web-default',
  }, { expiresIn: '7d' });

  // Store refresh token hash for revocation capability
  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      id: jti,
      userId: user.id,
      tokenHash: crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex'),
      deviceId: deviceId || 'web-default',
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 3600 * 1000),
    },
  });

  // Refresh token in httpOnly cookie — never accessible to JS
  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', refreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true,
    secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production',
    sameSite: 'strict',
    path: '/api/auth',
    maxAge: 7 * 24 * 3600,
  });

  return { accessToken, user: { id: user.id, name: user.name, roles: user.roles } };
});

// ==========================================
// 2. Token Verification — auth hook
// ==========================================
import fp from 'fastify-plugin';

export default fp(async function authPlugin(fastify) {
  fastify.decorateRequest('user', null);

  fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
    // Skip public routes
    if (req.routeOptions?.config?.isPublic) return;

    try {
      const decoded = await req.jwtVerify();
      if (decoded.type !== 'access') {
        return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid token type' });
      }
      req.user = decoded;
    } catch (err) {
      if (err.code === 'FST_JWT_AUTHORIZATION_TOKEN_EXPIRED') {
        return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Token expired', code: 'TOKEN_EXPIRED' });
      }
      return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
    }
  });
});

// ==========================================
// 3. Token Renewal — refresh endpoint
// ==========================================
app.post('/api/auth/refresh', {
  config: { isPublic: true }, // skip auth hook
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (!refreshToken) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'No refresh token' });
  }

  let decoded;
  try {
    decoded = app.jwt.verify(refreshToken);
    if (decoded.type !== 'refresh') throw new Error('Wrong type');
  } catch {
    reply.clearCookie('refreshToken', { path: '/api/auth' });
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid refresh token' });
  }

  // Verify token exists in DB (not revoked)
  const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
  const stored = await prisma.refreshToken.findUnique({ where: { id: decoded.jti } });

  if (!stored || stored.tokenHash !== tokenHash) {
    // Token reuse detected — possible theft. Revoke ALL user tokens.
    await prisma.refreshToken.deleteMany({ where: { userId: decoded.sub } });
    reply.clearCookie('refreshToken', { path: '/api/auth' });
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Token reuse detected — all sessions revoked' });
  }

  // Rotate: delete old, create new pair
  await prisma.refreshToken.delete({ where: { id: stored.id } });

  // Fetch latest user data (roles may have changed)
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: decoded.sub } });
  if (!user) return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'User not found' });

  const newAccessToken = app.jwt.sign({
    sub: user.id, roles: user.roles, department: user.department, type: 'access',
  }, { expiresIn: '15m' });

  const newJti = crypto.randomUUID();
  const newRefreshToken = app.jwt.sign({
    sub: user.id, type: 'refresh', jti: newJti, deviceId: decoded.deviceId,
  }, { expiresIn: '7d' });

  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      id: newJti,
      userId: user.id,
      tokenHash: crypto.createHash('sha256').update(newRefreshToken).digest('hex'),
      deviceId: decoded.deviceId,
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 3600 * 1000),
    },
  });

  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true, secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production',
    sameSite: 'strict', path: '/api/auth', maxAge: 7 * 24 * 3600,
  });

  return { accessToken: newAccessToken };
});

// ==========================================
// 4. Revocation — logout & password change
// ==========================================
app.post('/api/auth/logout', async (req, reply) => {
  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (refreshToken) {
    const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
    await prisma.refreshToken.deleteMany({ where: { tokenHash } });
  }
  reply.clearCookie('refreshToken', { path: '/api/auth' });
  return { message: 'Logged out' };
});

// Logout from all devices
app.post('/api/auth/logout-all', async (req, reply) => {
  await prisma.refreshToken.deleteMany({ where: { userId: req.user.sub } });
  reply.clearCookie('refreshToken', { path: '/api/auth' });
  return { message: 'All sessions revoked' };
});

// ==========================================
// 5. Socket.io Auth — handshake verification
// ==========================================
import { Server } from 'socket.io';

const io = new Server(app.server, {
  cors: { origin: process.env.CLIENT_URL, credentials: true },
});

io.use((socket, next) => {
  const token = socket.handshake.auth?.token;
  if (!token) return next(new Error('Authentication required'));
  try {
    const decoded = app.jwt.verify(token);
    if (decoded.type !== 'access') throw new Error('Wrong token type');
    socket.data.user = decoded;
    // Join user-specific room for targeted notifications
    socket.join(`user:${decoded.sub}`);
    socket.join(`dept:${decoded.department}`);
    next();
  } catch {
    next(new Error('Invalid token'));
  }
});

// Emit leave approval notification to specific user
function notifyLeaveApproval(employeeId, leaveData) {
  io.to(`user:${employeeId}`).emit('leave:approved', leaveData);
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj encountered a production issue where Socket.io connections dropped every 15 minutes because the access token used during the handshake expired. When the socket reconnected automatically, it reused the stale token from the initial connection, causing an authentication loop.

Debugging steps:

  1. Symptom: Managers stopped receiving real-time leave approval notifications after ~15 minutes of being on the dashboard.
  2. Investigation: Socket.io server logs showed Authentication required errors coinciding with access token expiry time. The Angular socket service was initialized with the token at login time and never updated.
  3. Root cause: The Socket.io client's auth option was set once during initialization. When the socket disconnected and attempted auto-reconnect, it sent the same expired token.
  4. Fix: Changed the Socket.io client configuration to use a function for auth that always returns the latest access token from the auth service's in-memory state. Also added a server-side allowEIO3 option and a socket.on('token:refresh') event that lets the client push a new token mid-connection without reconnecting.
  5. Validation: Wrote a Playwright test that logs in, connects to Socket.io, advances the clock by 16 minutes (triggering a token refresh), and verifies the socket remains connected and can receive events.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Refresh token reuse after rotation goes undetected — stolen tokens used in parallelImplement automatic reuse detection: if a rotated-out token is used again, revoke ALL of that user's sessions and force re-login. Log the event for security audit.
JWT claims become stale — user's role changed but old token still has old roleKeep access token TTL short (15m). On refresh, always re-read user roles from DB before signing new access token. For critical role changes, admin can trigger /logout-all for the user.
Socket.io reconnection uses expired access tokenUse a function-based auth config that reads the latest token. Add a token:refresh socket event for mid-session token updates without reconnection.
Refresh token DB table grows unbounded with expired entriesRun a scheduled Prisma cleanup: prisma.refreshToken.deleteMany({ where: { expiresAt: { lt: new Date() } } }) via a daily cron job or Fastify onReady hook with setInterval.
JWT secret leaked in logs or error messagesNever log token payloads in production. Use structured logging with field redaction. Store JWT secret in environment variables, never in code. Rotate secrets using a two-key verification strategy (verify with both old and new key during rotation window).

7. Validation Strategy

  • Full lifecycle E2E test (Playwright): Login → make API call → wait for access token expiry → make another call → verify silent refresh happened (network tab shows /auth/refresh 200) → verify the retried call succeeded
  • Revocation test: Login on two browser contexts, logout from context A, verify context B's next API call triggers refresh → refresh fails (token revoked) → redirect to login
  • Token reuse detection test: Capture a refresh token, use it to refresh (get new pair), then replay the old refresh token — verify server revokes ALL sessions and returns 401 with a reuse warning
  • Socket.io continuity test: Connect socket, advance clock past access token expiry, emit an event from server → verify client receives it (socket reconnected with fresh token)
  • Security headers test: Verify refresh token cookie has httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict flags. Verify access token is NOT in cookies (only in Authorization header)
  • Cleanup job test: Insert expired refresh tokens, trigger cleanup, verify they're deleted and active tokens are preserved
